file_name: G and V - 2021 - Skin cancer detection using support vector machine with histogram of oriented gradients features.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a machine learning approach for skin cancer detection. Specifically, it employs Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) for feature extraction and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel for classification on the ISIC 2018 dermatoscopic image dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper explicitly states in the abstract that it 'proposes an efficient skin cancer detection technique' and describes original experimental work involving the ISIC 2018 dataset, HOG feature extraction, and SVM classification. It presents new results, including specific performance metrics (76% accuracy, 85% specificity, etc.) and a confusion matrix, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: SKIN CANCER DETECTION USING SUPPORT VECTOR MACHINE WITH HISTOGRAM OF ORIENTED GRADIENTS FEATURES
brief_summary: This study proposes an efficient skin cancer detection technique using Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel. The researchers utilized the ISIC 2018 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven skin cancer classes, including Melanoma and Melanocytic Nevus. The methodology involved converting images to grayscale, applying a median filter for noise reduction, and using random resampling to rebalance the imbalanced class distribution. HOG features were extracted from the preprocessed images and classified by the SVM model to predict cancer class labels. The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 76%, specificity of 85%, precision of 84%, recall of 76%, and an F1-score of 75%. The results indicate that the SVM with HOG features outperformed existing methods like Random Forest and KNN, showing competitively higher sensitivity and specificity values.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven skin cancer classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification. Initial count: 1113 images.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification to detect skin cancer types. The model predicts one of seven discrete classes: Melanoma (MEL), Melanocytic Nevus (NV), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Actinic Keratosis (AKIEC), Benign Keratosis (BKL), Dermatofibroma (DF), and Vascular Lesion (VASC). The task is explicitly described as 'detecting cancer class labels' using SVM classification on HOG features from the ISIC 2018 dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2018)
datasets_used.description: A public dermatoscopic image dataset containing 10,015 images of seven skin cancer types. The dataset was used as the primary source for training and testing the proposed SVM with HOG classification model.

use.training: The dataset was split into training and testing sets. The training set (4,741 images after resampling) was used to train the SVM classifier with HOG features.
use.testing: The testing set (5,274 images) was used to evaluate the performance of the model using metrics such as accuracy, specificity, precision, recall, and F1-score.
use.other: The dataset was subjected to random undersampling (for the majority class NV) and random oversampling (for minority classes) to rebalance class distribution before feature extraction and classification.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate and report performance metrics including accuracy, specificity, precision, recall, and F1-score for the SVM with HOG classification model.
To rebalance the imbalanced class distribution in the ISIC 2018 dataset prior to feature extraction and classification, ensuring equal class sizes (Average Sample Size) for training.
To compare the performance of the proposed SVM with HOG method against existing methods (SVM without HOG, Random Forest, KNN) in terms of sensitivity and specificity.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, specificity, etc.) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018.notes: The proposed SVM with HOG method achieved 76% accuracy, 85% specificity, 84% precision, 76% recall, and 75% F1-score. This performance was competitively 2% and 9% higher in sensitivity and specificity values, respectively, compared to existing methods (Random Forest, KNN, Greedy Fusion, Linear SVM Fusion, Nonlinear SVM Fusion). The SVM without HOG baseline showed lower performance with an average accuracy of 0.67 and average specificity of 0.85.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel

architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Horizontal and vertical gradients calculated by filtering with horizontal and vertical kernels; gradient magnitude and orientation calculated; histogram of gradients computed to extract feature descriptor
architecture_details.kernel_function: Radial Basis Function (RBF) defined as k(x, x') = exp(-||x - x'||^2 / (2*sigma^2))
architecture_details.decision_boundary: Multiple hyperplanes generated to detect cancer class labels
architecture_details.preprocessing: Conversion to grayscale (3D to 1D pixel values), median filter for noise reduction, random undersampling for majority class (NV) and random oversampling for minority classes to rebalance distribution (Average Sample Size = 1431)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel
preprocessing_techniques: Conversion of color images (3D pixel values) to grayscale (1D pixel values) to reduce computational complexity
Application of a median filter to remove unnecessary noise and reduce intensity variance
Random undersampling of the majority class (Melanocytic Nevus) to rebalance class distribution
Random oversampling of minority classes to rebalance class distribution
Splitting the dataset into training and testing sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the majority class (Melanocytic Nevus) to reduce its sample size to the Average Sample Size (ASS) of 1431.
Applied to minority classes (MEL, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC) to increase their sample sizes to the Average Sample Size (ASS) of 1431.

conclusions: The proposed method using Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) features achieved an accuracy of 76%, specificity of 85%, precision of 84%, recall of 76%, and an F1-score of 75% on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

The proposed SVM with HOG method demonstrated competitively higher sensitivity (2% higher) and specificity (9% higher) values compared to existing methods including Random Forest, KNN, Greedy Fusion, Linear SVM Fusion, and Nonlinear SVM Fusion.
The SVM with HOG technique outperformed the standard SVM method (without HOG features), which achieved an average accuracy of 0.67 and average specificity of 0.85.

The study confirms that HOG feature extraction combined with SVM classification is an efficient technique for detecting skin cancer class labels in dermatoscopic images, addressing limitations of manual and semi-automated methods such as high time consumption and lower accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which is known to have significant class imbalance (e.g., Melanocytic Nevus is the majority class with 6705 images vs. Dermatofibroma with 115 images).

To address class imbalance, the authors applied random undersampling to the majority class and random oversampling to minority classes, resulting in an equal sample size of 1431 per class for training. This aggressive resampling strategy may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence.

The reported accuracy of 76% is relatively low for a multi-class classification task involving seven distinct skin lesion types, suggesting limited discriminative power of HOG features combined with SVM for this specific problem.

The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to validate the claimed competitive superiority (2% higher sensitivity, 9% higher specificity) over baseline methods like Random Forest and KNN.

The methodology involves converting color images to grayscale, which discards color information that is often critical for dermatological diagnosis (e.g., distinguishing melanoma from benign nevi based on color variegation).

The model was trained and tested on a single public dataset (ISIC 2018) without external validation on independent datasets, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or imaging devices.

The confusion matrix reveals that the model misclassified several Melanoma cases as Benign Keratosis (8 false negatives) and Dermatofibroma (7 false negatives), which has serious clinical implications for cancer detection.

The paper compares the proposed method against older or less standard baselines (e.g., Greedy Fusion, Linear SVM Fusion) rather than state-of-the-art deep learning models (e.g., CNNs), making the performance comparison less comprehensive for current standards.
